mnemonics.title: FDM - Frequency Divides Frequency
mnemonics.content: Frequency Division Multiplexing: Different signals get different frequency bands. Think 'Frequent Data Moved' - signals travel at different frequencies simultaneously without interfering.

mnemonics.title: TDM - Time Does More
mnemonics.content: Time Division Multiplexing: Multiple signals share the same frequency but take turns in time slots. Remember 'Time Divides Many' - signals take different time slices.

mnemonics.title: WDM - Waves Dance Mix
mnemonics.content: Wavelength Division Multiplexing: Different wavelengths (colors) of light carry data simultaneously in fiber optics. 'Wide Data Moved' through different light colors.

mnemonics.title: MUX-DEMUX Match
mnemonics.content: MUX combines (multiplexes) signals at sender, DEMUX separates (demultiplexes) at receiver. Remember: 'Many Users X' (combine) → send → 'Democracy X' (separate for each).

mnemonics.title: CDM - Codes Do Matter
mnemonics.content: Code Division Multiplexing: Each signal gets a unique code to distinguish it from others. Remember 'Code Divides Messages' - signals spread across same frequency with unique codes.

acronyms.usage: Use when multiple analog signals need simultaneous transmission on different frequency bands (e.g., radio stations, TV channels)
Use when digital signals take turns sharing a single channel in fixed time slots (e.g., telephone networks, digital audio)
Use in fiber optics when multiple light wavelengths carry data simultaneously (e.g., high-speed internet backbone)
Use in wireless communications where all signals share same frequency but use unique codes (e.g., 3G mobile, GPS)
Device at sender end that combines multiple signals into one transmission channel
Device at receiver end that separates combined signals back into individual channels
Use when time slots are allocated dynamically based on demand (more efficient than synchronous TDM)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a multi-lane highway where each lane (frequency band) carries different vehicles (signals) simultaneously. All travel together but stay in their own lane - no crashing, just different frequencies on different lanes.
Picture people (signals) sitting at a round table taking turns speaking. Only one person speaks at a time, but everyone gets a time slot. The microphone (channel) is shared in rotation.
Imagine a prism splitting white light into rainbow colors. Reverse it: many different colored laser beams (different wavelengths) traveling together through one fiber optic cable without mixing.

Think of a funnel: many small streams (input signals) pour in from the wide top, combine into one powerful stream (output) at the narrow bottom - that's MUX. DEMUX is the reverse: one stream splits into many.

Imagine a party where everyone speaks at once but in different languages (codes). Each person only understands their language, so the noise becomes organized communication - signals spread across same frequency with unique codes.

analogies.analogy: Postal System with Combined Packages: Instead of sending 10 separate letters, you bundle them into one envelope (multiplexing). The postal service carries one package (channel) but it contains multiple letters (signals). At the destination, the envelope is opened and each letter goes to its intended recipient (demultiplexing).

Radio Station Frequencies: Different radio stations (Radio Mirchi, FM Gold) broadcast simultaneously on different frequencies. Your radio tuner (demultiplexer) selects one frequency to receive that specific station. All stations exist in the air at once without干扰.

Traffic Lights at Intersection: At a busy intersection, vehicles from different directions take turns moving. Green light gives time slot to one direction, then yellow, then red - another direction gets its turn. The road (channel) is shared by rotating time slots.

Fiber Optic as Multi-Lane Light Highway: Just like highway lane markers separate cars, different colors of light (red, green, blue, infrared) travel through a single fiber without mixing. Each color carries different data, like lanes carrying different vehicles.

Phone Conversations in Noisy Room: Imagine 10 people talking simultaneously in one room, but each pair uses a different language (Hindi, English, Tamil, etc.). Each listener only understands their language and filters out others - all conversations exist together but are distinguishable by their unique code.

Train Station Platform: At a major station (MUX), passengers from many platforms (input signals) board one long train (combined signal). At destination (DEMUX), passengers disembark to their respective platforms (individual signals). Same train, different destinations.
